patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 62-year-old woman, anxious but clear. Open with: “I keep getting sudden electric-shock pains on the right side of my face.” Volunteer: brief stabbing shocks in right cheek/jaw lasting seconds, occurring in bursts; triggered by touching your face or brushing teeth; you avoid chewing on that side out of fear. If asked, add: breeze/washing face can trigger; between attacks you’re fine but on edge; always one-sided; no fever, swelling, facial droop, or speech trouble. Only reveal if specifically probed: a small trigger “spot” near upper lip/cheek; eating less/weight loss; poor sleep; skipping dental care and brushing less on that side. If the clinician explores mood/relationships/meds, admit you avoid kissing/close contact, feel down/anxious, and you’ve been taking more OTC pain pills than recommended. Share misconceptions: thought it was a tooth or sinus issue (eye waters); tried leftover antibiotics without benefit; wondered about grinding/jaw tension. If shown empathy, soften, say you’re scared and worried about costs and medication side effects affecting work and bills.

medications: Carbamazepine 200 mg PO three times daily
Amlodipine 5 mg PO once daily
Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg PO once daily
Atorvastatin 20 mg PO nightly
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3 g/day)
confounders: Hyponatremia likely medication-related (carbamazepine-associated SIADH and/or thiazide effect), which can cause dizziness/fatigue and complicate symptom attribution
Recent dental work (molar filling 3 weeks ago) with intermittent jaw discomfort, raising concern for odontogenic pain vs trigeminal neuralgia
History of migraine without aura causing episodic unilateral head/facial pain that may mimic neuralgia features
Dry mouth and dental sensitivity that can trigger facial pain episodes and complicate identification of classic trigger zones

correct_diagnosis.primary: Trigeminal neuralgia
required_evidence: Recurrent brief paroxysms of unilateral facial pain in trigeminal distribution (V1/V2/V3)
Pain is severe, electric shock-like/shooting/stabbing in quality
Attacks last from a fraction of a second up to 2 minutes
Triggered by innocuous stimuli (e.g., light touch, chewing, talking, brushing teeth, cold air) with identifiable trigger zones
No objective neurological deficit on exam (if present, consider secondary cause)
Between attacks, patient is typically pain-free (or minimal background pain)
No features more consistent with alternative diagnoses (e.g., dental infection, sinusitis, temporal arteritis, migraine/cluster, postherpetic neuralgia)

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal at baseline (used to monitor for carbamazepine-associated leukopenia/agranulocytosis).
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal at baseline (used to monitor for carbamazepine-associated anemia).
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Typically normal at baseline (used to monitor for carbamazepine-associated thrombocytopenia).
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive metabolic panel - Sodium: Typically normal at baseline; monitor for hyponatremia (SIADH) risk with carbamazepine.

medications: Carbamazepine 100 mg PO twice daily, titrate by 100-200 mg/day every 3-7 days to effect (typical maintenance 200 mg PO three to four times daily; max 1200 mg/day)
non_pharmacological: Avoid/modify known triggers when possible (chewing on affected side, cold wind exposure, very hot/cold foods) while initiating therapy

Discuss need for evaluation for secondary causes if atypical features (sensory loss, bilateral symptoms, age <40, persistent pain between attacks); arrange MRI brain with attention to trigeminal nerve if indicated/available
If medication-refractory or intolerant: refer to neurology and discuss procedural options (microvascular decompression, percutaneous rhizotomy procedures, stereotactic radiosurgery)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 weeks for pain control, adverse effects, and dose titration; repeat CBC and sodium within 2-4 weeks after initiation/titration and periodically thereafter.

must_include: Condition involves irritation/misfiring of the trigeminal nerve causing brief, severe shock-like facial pain
Typical triggers and short attack duration are characteristic
Red flags that would suggest a secondary cause and warrant imaging (numbness/weakness, hearing/vision changes, persistent pain between attacks, bilateral symptoms, onset at younger age)
Carbamazepine is first-line and often highly effective; dose will be started low and increased gradually
Common side effects: dizziness, drowsiness, nausea, unsteadiness; advise caution with driving/operating machinery until effects known
Serious but rare risks: rash/SJS/TEN, low blood counts (infection signs), liver issues; seek urgent care for new rash, fever/sore throat, mouth sores, easy bruising/bleeding, jaundice
Need for lab monitoring (CBC and sodium) after starting and during dose changes
Review allergies (penicillin rash; codeine causes nausea/vomiting) and clarify no prior reaction to anticonvulsants
Discuss pregnancy considerations/contraception if applicable (carbamazepine is teratogenic and induces hepatic enzymes reducing hormonal contraceptive effectiveness)
Discuss interaction risks (CYP enzyme induction; check all current meds/supplements; avoid grapefruit; avoid excess alcohol/CNS depressants)
Keep a trigger/pain diary to guide avoidance and assess response to treatment
Maintain regular meals/hydration and oral hygiene while minimizing triggers (use lukewarm water, soft toothbrush if needed)
When to seek urgent evaluation: new neurological deficits, persistent severe pain not responding to titration, signs of severe medication reaction

Review baseline labs to identify contraindications/precautions (e.g., cytopenias, hepatic dysfunction, hyponatremia) prior to prescribing.

Critical safety check before prescribing: review current meds/supplements and interaction risks (CYP induction, contraceptive efficacy, grapefruit, CNS depressants).

Initiate first-line therapy (carbamazepine) with low-start/slow-titrate plan and monitoring plan; provide clear stop/seek-care instructions for serious adverse effects.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment expectations/side effects, monitoring, interactions, pregnancy/contraception considerations, lifestyle measures, and urgent return precautions.
Arrange timely follow-up for titration and lab monitoring (e.g., within 1–2 weeks) and earlier if worsening or adverse effects.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 36-year-old anxious but clear-speaking marketing project manager, 2 weeks postpartum. Start by saying you’ve had sudden “thunderclap” headaches that peak within seconds, feel like the worst of your life, and have come in bursts over the last week. Volunteer: photophobia, nausea, and that you want to lie still during attacks. If asked, describe occipital pain that can spread, often triggered by sex, straining, or hard workouts; between attacks you feel mostly okay but washed out and worried. If asked about neuro symptoms, report brief right-hand tingling/clumsiness, brief word-finding trouble, and one episode of blurry vision; say BP was high during an attack; deny fever/neck stiffness. Withhold unless directly asked: new migraine medicine, decongestant, lots of energy drinks, poor sleep postpartum. Be resistant/embarrassed: only admit cocaine use and borrowing a friend’s ADHD stimulant if asked directly and nonjudgmentally. Express misconceptions: you assumed it was a bad migraine or tension/pinched nerve; you fear an aneurysm. Raise cost/work concerns and ask for the cheapest option. If shown empathy, you soften, thank them, and become more willing to disclose.

medications: Sertraline 100 mg PO daily
Sumatriptan 50 mg PO PRN migraine (max 200 mg/day)
Combined oral contraceptive (ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel) 1 tablet PO daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO q6h PRN pain
confounders: Postpartum state (elevated risk for RCVS and also overlaps with preeclampsia-related headache/hypertension)
Migraine history with recent triptan use (thunderclap headache may be misattributed to migraine; triptans can precipitate/worsen vasoconstriction)
SSRI use (sertraline) as a potential precipitating vasoactive agent
Cannabis use (positive urine THC) associated with RCVS and can obscure etiology
Transient severe hypertension during headache (may suggest hypertensive emergency or posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS)
required_evidence: Recurrent thunderclap headaches reaching peak intensity within 1 minute, often over days to weeks
No aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage on initial evaluation (or hemorrhage pattern not consistent with aneurysmal SAH)

Cerebral arterial vasoconstriction on CTA/MRA/cerebral angiography (multifocal segmental narrowing/"string-of-beads") OR high clinical suspicion with plan for repeat vascular imaging in 1–2 weeks if initial imaging normal
Reversibility of vasoconstriction on follow-up imaging within ~12 weeks (for definitive diagnosis)
Assessment for common triggers (postpartum state; vasoactive drugs such as triptans, SSRIs/SNRIs, decongestants; cannabis/cocaine/amphetamines; exertion/sexual activity)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no anemia-related secondary headache clues)

medications: Nimodipine 60 mg PO every 4 hours
Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for headache (max 3 g/day if no liver disease)
Ondansetron 4 mg PO/IV every 8 hours as needed for nausea
non_pharmacological: Immediate evaluation for secondary causes of thunderclap headache and complications (neuroimaging and vascular imaging; monitor for stroke/ICH/SAH)
Avoid/stop potential triggers and vasoactive agents (triptans, ergot derivatives, sympathomimetic decongestants, cocaine/amphetamines, cannabis; review serotonergic agents case-by-case)
Blood pressure management: avoid severe hypertension; avoid hypotension that could worsen cerebral perfusion (individualize targets; inpatient monitoring if severe symptoms/neurologic deficits)
Admit/observe if severe recurrent thunderclap headaches, focal deficits, seizures, or imaging abnormalities; neurology consultation
Avoid glucocorticoids unless another clear indication (steroids may worsen outcomes in RCVS)
Hydration and rest; avoid intense exertion/sexual activity until stabilized
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up within 1 week; repeat CTA/MRA in 1–2 weeks if initial vascular imaging normal but suspicion remains, and confirm reversibility with repeat imaging by 8–12 weeks.

must_include: RCVS involves temporary narrowing/spasm of brain arteries causing sudden severe headaches
Diagnosis is supported by vascular imaging and confirmed by improvement/reversibility over weeks
Need to rule out emergencies that can mimic it (aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage, stroke)
Calcium-channel blocker (e.g., nimodipine) is used to reduce vasoconstriction and headache burden; evidence is mainly observational
Avoid steroids unless another diagnosis requires them
Avoid vasoactive headache meds like triptans/ergots and sympathomimetics
Discuss warning signs requiring urgent care: new weakness/numbness, trouble speaking, vision loss, seizure, confusion, persistent worst headache, fainting
Discuss expected course: many improve over days to weeks, but there is a risk of stroke/bleeding especially early
Shared plan for monitoring and follow-up imaging
Avoid triggers (recreational drugs, decongestants, intense exertion/sexual activity temporarily)
Review all current meds/supplements for vasoactive agents before restarting
Encourage adherence to follow-up and imaging to confirm reversibility

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit thunderclap headache features, timing/recurrent episodes, triggers (sex/exertion/Valsalva), associated neuro symptoms, postpartum state, and exposure to vasoactive substances/meds; screen for red flags suggesting SAH/stroke/meningitis.

Order initial evaluation to exclude mimics/complications and support safe prescribing (e.g., pregnancy test if relevant, basic labs); ensure emergent neuroimaging/vascular imaging is arranged if not already done.
Review imaging/labs to ensure emergent causes are ruled out (SAH, ischemic stroke, dissection) and that vascular imaging supports RCVS (multifocal segmental vasoconstriction).
State working diagnosis of RCVS (or probable RCVS) while acknowledging need for follow-up imaging to confirm reversibility and ongoing exclusion of mimics.
Critical safety check prior to any medication prescribing (e.g., calcium-channel blocker, analgesics, antiemetics).
Critical safety check to identify interactions (e.g., nimodipine with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers, additive hypotension with antihypertensives).
Critical safety check to confirm no contraindications (e.g., severe hypotension, significant aortic stenosis, cardiogenic shock, pregnancy considerations depending on drug choice).

Initiate symptom-directed therapy consistent with RCVS care (e.g., calcium-channel blocker such as nimodipine/verapamil; avoid triptans/ergots/sympathomimetics; avoid steroids unless another diagnosis warrants).
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment rationale/limitations, address concerns and red flags, and educate on trigger avoidance and medication review.
Arrange neurology follow-up and repeat vascular imaging to document reversibility; plan monitoring for early complications and medication side effects (e.g., hypotension).
Close after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and clear follow-up plan.

expected_lab_results.Urine protein/creatinine ratio (spot): Elevated if nephropathy present (commonly >0.2 mg/mg; nephrotic-range possible >2 mg/mg)
expected_lab_results.Urinalysis - blood: Often negative or trace; significant positivity suggests alternate/additional pathology
expected_lab_results.Urine microscopy - RBCs: 0–5 RBC/hpf typical; marked hematuria or dysmorphic RBCs would be atypical and prompt alternate workup
expected_lab_results.Serum albumin: Normal unless heavy proteinuria; may be low with nephrotic-range proteinuria

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 47-year-old anxious project manager. Open with: “I can’t sleep like I used to—most nights I’m awake for hours.” Speak tangentially, jumping from sleep to work stress, money, and your sleep-tracker. Volunteer: long time to fall asleep, frequent awakenings, can’t get back to sleep, daytime fog and exhaustion. If asked, give details: 3 months, 4–5 nights/week, ~4–5 hours total, racing thoughts, irritability, afternoon coffee, phone scrolling in bed, early waking, OTC sleep aid a few nights/week losing effect.

Do NOT mention unless specifically asked about snoring/breathing, morning symptoms, weight, reflux, nighttime urination, restless legs, or low mood: dry mouth, dull morning headache, loud snoring/pauses, weight gain, heartburn if eating late, getting up to pee, jumpy legs, less interest/low mood.

Act resistant about alcohol/cannabis and taking your partner’s prescription pill—minimize at first, admit if asked directly; worry about it “going on your record.” Express misconceptions: thyroid/hormones, “low blood sugar,” mattress/bad energy, panic about “no deep sleep.” Push for a cheap prescription; worry CBT-I/sleep study costs and time off work. If the clinician is empathic, soften, feel relieved, and share more.

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit sleep complaint details (onset, duration, frequency), sleep schedule, sleep latency/maintenance/early awakening, daytime impairment, and patient goals/prior treatments to establish insomnia criteria and impact.

Screen for contributors and differentials: mood/anxiety, substance use (caffeine/alcohol/cannabis), medication effects, shift work/circadian issues, RLS/PLMD symptoms, OSA risk (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, morning headaches), pain, and pregnancy status when relevant.

Make a diagnosis of insomnia (acute vs chronic based on duration/frequency) including requirement for daytime impairment, while noting suspected contributors (e.g., possible OSA/RLS/substance/medication effects).
Explain diagnosis and plan to rule out/identify contributors (thyroid disease, iron deficiency/RLS, sleep apnea risk, substance/medication effects).

Provide first-line treatment counseling: CBT-I as first-line and more durable than medications; introduce sleep diary, stimulus control, consistent schedule, caffeine cutoff, and alcohol effects on sleep maintenance.
Before any prescribing, verify allergy history; specifically account for penicillin causing hives and diphenhydramine causing paradoxical agitation to avoid OTC antihistamine sleep aids.
Before any prescribing, assess interactions with current meds/substances, especially other sedatives, alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, and CNS depressants.

If medication is appropriate after first-line counseling and safety checks, prescribe a guideline-consistent short-term option (or none), avoiding barbiturates (including aprobarbital); if patient is on sedatives, plan a gradual taper rather than abrupt cessation.

Arrange follow-up to review sleep diary/CBT-I progress, reassess daytime impairment and safety (driving), monitor adverse effects/withdrawal if tapering, and adjust plan; consider referral for CBT-I or sleep study if OSA risk is significant.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

expected_lab_results.Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): May be elevated (stress response) or low if severe infection/bone marrow suppression; neutropenia increases risk and escalates urgency
expected_lab_results.Absolute lymphocyte count: Often low/abnormal in combined immunodeficiency; may be low in severe infection
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: May be normal or mildly decreased (anemia of inflammation or hemodilution)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: May be decreased in sepsis (consumption) or normal early; falling trend is concerning
expected_lab_results.Procalcitonin: Elevated, supporting bacterial infection (not definitive alone)
expected_lab_results.Serum lactate: May be elevated (>=2 mmol/L concerning; higher suggests hypoperfusion/septic shock)

medications: Ceftazidime 2 g IV every 8 hours (adjust for renal function)
Human immune globulin (IVIG) 400-600 mg/kg IV once, then repeat every 3-4 weeks to maintain adequate IgG trough (dose individualized to levels/infections)
non_pharmacological: Immediate sepsis management: obtain blood cultures (and other source cultures as indicated) before antibiotics if this does not delay therapy
Early IV crystalloid resuscitation for hypotension or lactate elevation (e.g., 30 mL/kg) and reassess perfusion frequently
Source identification and control (focused exam and targeted imaging/consults as indicated; remove/replace infected lines if suspected)
Close monitoring of vitals, urine output, mental status; escalate level of care (ICU) if shock, rising lactate, or organ dysfunction
Repeat lactate if initially elevated; trend CBC and inflammatory markers to assess response
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1 hour for sepsis response and hemodynamics; repeat labs (CBC, CRP/procalcitonin as clinically useful, lactate if elevated) within 4-6 hours; daily review of antibiotic choice with culture results and clinical course

must_include: You have a primary immune system condition (DOCK8-related hyper-IgE syndrome) that makes infections more severe and recurrent

IVIG is used to support low IgG and reduce infection risk; dosing and schedule are individualized
Review antibiotic allergies: vancomycin caused infusion-related flushing/pruritus (not IgE-mediated anaphylaxis) and amoxicillin caused a delayed rash
Discuss what reactions to watch for with ceftazidime and when to seek urgent help (hives, breathing difficulty, facial swelling, severe rash)
Explain that antibiotics may be changed based on cultures and response

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX’s anxious parent. Speak vaguely at first and worry out loud. Start with: “He gets fevers out of nowhere every few weeks and he’s always tired.” Volunteer: pale most days, gets colds/ear infections often and they last longer, falling behind in school and needs special help.

If asked about fever episodes, add: body aches, headache, wants to lie down, sometimes mouth sores, poor appetite and mild belly discomfort. If asked about bleeding/bruising, mention easy bruising and tiny red/purple dots on legs after sickness. If asked about breathing/exercise, say he gets short of breath on stairs and can’t keep up in gym. If asked about growth, say he’s growing slower.

Do NOT mention unless directly asked: constant thirst/peeing at night, salty cravings, dizziness on standing, cramps/weakness in heat, bone/shin pain, occasional fast heartbeat, dark circles/headaches.

Be resistant/embarrassed if asked about bedwetting or stool accidents during fevers; minimize and try to change topic. Express misconceptions: “Maybe it’s viruses/teething,” worry about juvenile arthritis, ask if it’s just iron deficiency like a cousin. Bring up cost concerns and ask for fewer tests/visits or OTC iron. If clinician is empathetic, soften, share more, and admit you sometimes miss follow-ups because you feel judged.

diagnosis.primary: congenital sideroblastic anemia-B-cell immunodeficiency-periodic fever-developmental delay syndrome
differentials: juvenile arthritis due to defect in LACC1

correct_diagnosis.primary: congenital sideroblastic anemia-B-cell immunodeficiency-periodic fever-developmental delay syndrome
acceptable_alternatives: juvenile arthritis due to defect in LACC1 (LACC1-associated systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis/autoinflammation)
required_evidence: CBC pattern consistent with chronic congenital anemia (low hemoglobin/hematocrit with low RBC count)
microcytosis or low-normal MCV with elevated RDW suggestive of congenital sideroblastic anemia phenotype
inappropriately low/normal reticulocyte count for degree of anemia (hypoproliferative anemia)
history of periodic/recurrent febrile episodes without clear infectious source (periodic fever phenotype)
evidence/history of humoral immunodeficiency (recurrent sinopulmonary infections, poor vaccine responses, or known low immunoglobulins) consistent with B-cell immunodeficiency component
developmental delay documented in history/records (neurodevelopmental component)
exclusion of common acquired causes of microcytic anemia (iron deficiency/thalassemia) by prior workup or history if available

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Low (chronic anemia, often moderate to severe depending on baseline)
expected_lab_results.RBC count: Low or low-normal (not elevated as in many thalassemias)
expected_lab_results.MCV: Low or low-normal (microcytic or borderline microcytic)
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Low or inappropriately normal for degree of anemia (hypoproliferative)
expected_lab_results.WBC: Often normal between episodes; may be elevated during febrile/inflammatory episodes
expected_lab_results.Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): Typically normal; if cyclic hematopoiesis were present, ANC would show periodic severe nadirs

medications: Packed red blood cells 10-15 mL/kg IV once (as needed for symptomatic anemia or Hb below patient-specific transfusion threshold)
Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) 50 mg PO daily (trial; titrate per hematology based on response and tolerability)
Acetaminophen 10-15 mg/kg PO/PR every 4-6 hours as needed for fever (max 75 mg/kg/day; max 4 g/day)
Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for inflammatory pain/fever (avoid if significant thrombocytopenia/renal risk; max 40 mg/kg/day)
non_pharmacological: Hematology consultation for congenital sideroblastic anemia management (transfusion strategy, iron monitoring, consideration of chelation if transfusion-dependent)
Monitor iron overload if transfused (serial ferritin and liver iron assessment per hematology); initiate chelation if indicated by guideline-based thresholds
Immunology consultation for B-cell immunodeficiency evaluation and management (quantitative immunoglobulins, vaccine response testing, B-cell phenotyping)
Consider immunoglobulin replacement therapy (IVIG/SCIG) if hypogammaglobulinemia with impaired antibody responses and/or recurrent serious infections (per immunology)
Fever plan: prompt evaluation for infection with any fever given immunodeficiency risk; low threshold for cultures and empiric antibiotics guided by clinical status and local protocols
Developmental services: referral to early intervention/therapy (PT/OT/speech) and neurodevelopmental follow-up
If cyclic hematopoiesis remains a concern: obtain serial CBC/ANC 2-3 times weekly for 6-8 weeks to assess periodicity
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology and immunology follow-up within 2-4 weeks; earlier (24-72 hours) if ongoing fever, worsening symptoms, or post-transfusion reassessment needed

must_include: Condition combines a congenital anemia (sideroblastic-type) with immune system weakness (B-cell/humoral immunodeficiency) and recurrent fever episodes
Why labs support anemia and why additional immune testing may be needed
Differentials considered (autoinflammatory arthritis from LACC1 and cyclic neutropenia) and what would make them more/less likely
Transfusions are used when anemia is symptomatic or severe; discuss benefits and risks (reactions, iron overload)
Vitamin supplementation plan (folate; B6 trial) and that response may vary
Plan for immunology-directed infection prevention (possible immunoglobulin replacement; prompt fever evaluation)

Review antibiotic allergies: amoxicillin-clavulanate caused urticarial rash; vancomycin caused infusion-related reaction (red man syndrome) and can often be managed with slow infusion/premedication if ever necessary
Clear return precautions: fever, lethargy, breathing difficulty, poor intake, signs of severe anemia (syncope, chest pain, marked fatigue) warrant urgent evaluation
Set expectations for specialist follow-up and need for ongoing monitoring
Infection-risk mitigation: hand hygiene, avoiding sick contacts when possible, staying up to date on vaccines as recommended by immunology (may differ due to immune status)
Keep a fever log (timing, duration, associated symptoms) to help characterize periodicity
Adherence to follow-up labs/appointments for anemia monitoring and to detect complications like iron overload

Interpret results to confirm sideroblastic/congenital anemia pattern, assess for neutropenia/cyclic pattern, evaluate immune deficits, and identify immediate safety issues (severe anemia, neutropenia, infection).

Synthesize findings to diagnose congenital sideroblastic anemia–B-cell immunodeficiency–periodic fever–developmental delay syndrome and explicitly address key differentials (LACC1-associated autoinflammatory arthritis; cyclic neutropenia) with supporting/refuting features.
Explain diagnosis in plain language, why labs support anemia and why immune testing is needed, and discuss differential reasoning per communication milestones.

Noncritical but expected interaction check before prescribing supportive meds (e.g., pyridoxine/folate/iron chelation if applicable) and any antimicrobial plan.

Initiate treatment plan: vitamin supplementation (folate; trial of B6/pyridoxine), outline transfusion thresholds/indications, and immunology-directed infection prevention plan (e.g., consider immunoglobulin replacement via specialist; fever action plan).

If transfusions are used or ferritin elevated, order monitoring for iron overload (serial ferritin, transferrin saturation; consider liver iron assessment per specialty), and schedule periodic CBC/retic to track anemia and detect neutropenia patterns.

Address concerns: review antibiotic allergy nuances (Augmentin urticaria; vancomycin red man syndrome management), provide clear return precautions, and counsel lifestyle/infection-risk mitigation and fever log.
Arrange hematology and immunology follow-up, set expectations for ongoing monitoring, and ensure timely reassessment of anemia severity, infection risk, and developmental supports.
Close after confirming understanding, providing written/structured plan, and ensuring safety-net instructions.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA)
acceptable_alternatives: Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (HHS) with ketosis (mixed DKA/HHS)
required_evidence: Elevated anion gap metabolic acidosis (low bicarbonate with high anion_gap)
Positive ketosis (elevated beta_hydroxybutyrate)
Hyperglycemia (serum_glucose typically >250 mg/dL; may be lower in euglycemic DKA)
Electrolyte derangements consistent with DKA (total body potassium deficit; sodium may be low due to hyperglycemia)

expected_lab_results.serum_glucose: Elevated (typically >250 mg/dL; may be 150–250 mg/dL in euglycemic DKA)
expected_lab_results.hemoglobin_a1c: Often elevated if chronic hyperglycemia/undiagnosed diabetes; may be normal if new-onset
expected_lab_results.sodium: Low to normal (pseudo-hyponatremia from hyperglycemia); corrected sodium may be normal/high
expected_lab_results.potassium: Normal to high initially despite total body deficit; may be low if severe depletion
expected_lab_results.chloride: Often normal to low initially; may rise with saline resuscitation
expected_lab_results.bicarbonate: Low (<=18 mEq/L; often <15 in moderate-severe DKA)

medications: Regular insulin 0.1 units/kg IV bolus then 0.1 units/kg/hour IV infusion (or 0.14 units/kg/hour IV infusion without bolus) titrate to glucose fall 50-75 mg/dL/hour
Potassium chloride 20-30 mEq IV per liter of fluid to maintain serum potassium 4.0-5.0 mEq/L (hold insulin and give K+ first if K+ <3.3 mEq/L)
Dextrose 5% in 0.45% sodium chloride IV when serum glucose <200 mg/dL (continue insulin until anion gap closes and bicarbonate normalizes)
non_pharmacological: Initial isotonic fluid resuscitation with 0.9% sodium chloride 15-20 mL/kg IV in first hour, then adjust to 0.45% vs 0.9% based on corrected sodium and hemodynamics
Frequent monitoring: bedside glucose hourly; BMP/electrolytes, anion gap, bicarbonate every 2-4 hours; strict I&O; continuous cardiac monitoring
Identify and treat precipitating cause (e.g., infection, missed insulin, MI); obtain appropriate studies per suspected trigger
Transition to subcutaneous basal-bolus insulin when anion gap closed, patient eating, and bicarbonate/acid-base improved; overlap IV insulin with first basal dose by 1-2 hours
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1 hour after starting fluids/insulin; then ongoing ICU/step-down monitoring with labs every 2-4 hours until anion gap closed; outpatient diabetes follow-up within 1-2 weeks after discharge

must_include: DKA is caused by insulin deficiency leading to ketone buildup and blood acidity
This is a medical emergency requiring IV fluids, insulin, and close monitoring
Explain key lab abnormalities: high glucose, high ketones, low bicarbonate/high anion gap
Fluids first to treat dehydration and improve perfusion
IV insulin to stop ketone production; continue until anion gap closes (not just until glucose normalizes)
Potassium monitoring/replacement is essential; insulin can lower potassium and cause dangerous arrhythmias
Dextrose is added once glucose falls to allow continued insulin to clear ketones
Discuss risks: low blood sugar, low potassium, fluid overload; how these are monitored and prevented
Clarify expected course and need for frequent blood tests and possibly ICU-level care
Invite questions and assess understanding
Sick-day rules: never stop basal insulin, monitor glucose/ketones, maintain hydration, seek care for vomiting or persistent hyperglycemia
Medication adherence and follow-up for diabetes education
Warning signs of recurrence (nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain, rapid breathing, confusion)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit acute symptoms, diabetes history, triggers (infection/insulin omission), and assess for red flags (altered mental status, dehydration, Kussmaul breathing) to guide urgent workup.

Order diagnostic and severity labs for suspected DKA: bedside glucose, BMP/electrolytes, venous/arterial blood gas, serum/urine ketones (beta-hydroxybutyrate), CBC, lactate if concern for sepsis, magnesium/phosphate, UA, pregnancy test if applicable, and ECG; consider cultures/chest imaging if infection suspected.
Confirm DKA and quantify severity using glucose, ketones, pH/bicarbonate, anion gap, and potassium; identify precipitants and complications.
Make a working diagnosis of DKA (high anion gap metabolic acidosis with ketonemia/ketonuria and hyperglycemia) and document severity/need for ICU-level monitoring.
Perform non-critical safety check prior to prescribing insulin, fluids/electrolytes, antiemetics, or antibiotics if indicated.
Check for relevant interactions (e.g., potassium with ACEi/ARB, QT-prolonging antiemetics, insulin with beta-blockers masking hypoglycemia), if any home meds are present.

Initiate evidence-based DKA treatment: IV isotonic fluids first, then IV insulin infusion with protocolized potassium monitoring/repletion; add dextrose when glucose falls while continuing insulin until anion gap closes; address precipitating cause.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment rationale/sequence, risks and monitoring, expected course/ICU need, and sick-day/lifestyle prevention education; assess understanding and invite questions.
Arrange diabetes education and close outpatient follow-up after stabilization (endocrinology/primary care), including insulin regimen review and sick-day plan.
Close encounter after ensuring emergent management is initiated and patient understanding/follow-up plan are addressed.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 29-year-old elementary school teacher. Be cooperative and clear. Start with: “I keep tripping over my feet, and my ankles feel weak.” Volunteer early that you trip on curbs/uneven ground, your foot sometimes “flops” down, your feet look more hollow with curled toes, and your lower legs look skinnier. If asked, add: can’t run like before, fatigue with long walks, frequent ankle sprains/rolling, mild hand weakness (jars/buttons), occasional tingling in feet, and that this has slowly worsened over years (not sudden). Do not mention unless specifically asked: decreased sensation to floor/temperature, high-stepping gait, difficulty heel-walking/standing on heels, and uneven shoe wear. Only if asked about bladder/bowel or urgency, reluctantly admit a few near-accidents because you can’t hurry. Offer misconceptions: worry it’s a pinched back nerve or “bad circulation” because feet feel cold/tingly. correct_diagnosis.primary: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)
required_evidence: Chronic, slowly progressive distal muscle weakness and atrophy (especially feet/lower legs) with difficulty running, frequent ankle sprains, or foot drop
Distal sensory loss (reduced vibration/proprioception) in a length-dependent pattern
Pes cavus and/or hammer toes; high arches with calluses; possible gait abnormalities/steppage gait
Reduced or absent ankle reflexes
Family history of similar neuropathy or high suspicion of hereditary neuropathy
Electrodiagnostic evidence of peripheral neuropathy (demyelinating and/or axonal) OR genetic testing consistent with CMT (if available)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no anemia expected from CMT itself)

Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pain with food (avoid if CKD, GI bleed risk)
non_pharmacological: Refer to Neurology for confirmation (EMG/NCS) and consideration of genetic testing/counseling
Physical therapy for stretching, strengthening, balance training, and gait training
Occupational therapy for hand weakness/fine motor support and adaptive devices
Ankle-foot orthoses (AFO) for foot drop/ankle instability; supportive footwear and custom orthotics

Fall-risk assessment and home safety counseling; consider assistive device if unsteady
Avoid/limit known neurotoxic medications when possible (e.g., vincristine; caution with other neurotoxic agents)
Screen for and manage complications: neuropathic pain, recurrent ankle sprains, skin breakdown/ulcers from deformity

must_include: CMT is a hereditary peripheral nerve disorder causing slowly progressive weakness and sensory changes, usually starting in the feet/legs
Typical features include high arches/hammer toes, ankle weakness/foot drop, and reduced reflexes
Course is usually gradual; severity varies; many people remain ambulatory with supportive care
Discuss role of EMG/NCS and genetic testing to confirm type and inform family planning

PT/OT and braces/orthotics can improve gait, prevent falls, and reduce pain
Pain plan with safer first-line options and when to avoid NSAIDs (kidney disease, ulcer/bleeding risk)

Discuss inheritance patterns and offer genetic counseling if desired
Review red flags needing urgent evaluation: rapidly progressive weakness, new bowel/bladder dysfunction, severe new numbness, or inability to walk safely

Start with: “My hands and feet keep hurting and swelling, and my fingers look like they’re getting shorter.” Volunteer: aching/swelling in wrists and fingers most days; fingers look shorter/pulled in and rings don’t fit; foot/ankle pain with walking worse by day’s end; morning hand stiffness that improves with movement; small firm bumps near finger joints and elbows. correct_diagnosis.primary: Multicentric osteolysis, nodulosis, and arthropathy (MONA syndrome)
acceptable_alternatives: Multicentric osteolysis with arthropathy (hereditary osteolysis spectrum)
required_evidence: Clinical pattern of progressive osteolysis affecting carpal/tarsal bones with arthropathy and functional impairment (often childhood onset)
Subcutaneous nodules (nodulosis) in the setting of osteolysis/arthropathy
Inflammatory markers may be normal or mildly elevated; autoimmune serologies typically negative (RF and anti-CCP negative)
Radiographic evidence (if available) consistent with multicentric osteolysis rather than primary synovitis-driven erosions
Exclusion of infectious osteomyelitis and inflammatory arthritides based on history, exam, and available labs

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no consistent anemia); may be mildly low if chronic inflammation present
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal (may be mildly elevated if inflammation present)

medications: Naproxen 10 mg/kg/day PO divided BID PRN pain (max 1000 mg/day)
Acetaminophen 10-15 mg/kg PO q4-6h PRN pain/fever (max 75 mg/kg/day or 4000 mg/day, whichever lower)
non_pharmacological: Refer to pediatric/adult rheumatology (as age-appropriate) for confirmation and longitudinal management; consider genetics referral/testing (e.g., MMP2) and counseling
Physical and occupational therapy for joint protection, range-of-motion maintenance, strengthening, splinting/assistive devices, and function optimization
Orthopedic evaluation for deformity, instability, or severe functional limitation; consider imaging-guided care planning
Pain management plan emphasizing activity pacing, heat/cold as tolerated, and avoidance of high-impact activities that worsen pain or risk fractures
Screen for and manage complications: functional disability, contractures, foot/hand deformities; consider bone health assessment if clinically indicated
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess symptoms, function, and NSAID tolerability in 4-6 weeks; earlier (1-2 weeks) if worsening pain, swelling, fever, or new functional decline

must_include: This condition is a rare disorder causing progressive bone loss (osteolysis) and joint problems, often starting early in life
It can mimic inflammatory arthritis, but typical rheumatoid antibodies (RF/anti-CCP) are usually negative and the pattern is driven by osteolysis
Genetic evaluation may help confirm the diagnosis and guide family counseling
The goal is symptom control and preserving function; there may not be a single curative medication
Focus on pain control (e.g., NSAIDs/acetaminophen) and function (PT/OT, splints/assistive devices)
When to use PRN vs scheduled analgesics and how to avoid exceeding maximum daily doses
NSAID risks (stomach irritation/bleeding, kidney effects) and to take with food and avoid dehydration
Plan for specialist follow-up (rheumatology/genetics/orthopedics) and possible imaging to track osteolysis
Red flags requiring urgent care: fever, rapidly increasing redness/warmth, inability to bear weight/use limb, severe night pain, new neurologic symptoms
Set expectations about chronicity, monitoring, and the importance of therapy and protective strategies
Discuss uncertainty and the need to rule out mimics if the course or labs suggest infection or another inflammatory disease
Joint protection strategies and activity modification to reduce pain and injury risk
Adherence to PT/OT home exercises and use of splints/orthotics as recommended
Avoid combining multiple NSAIDs; limit alcohol (if applicable) and maintain hydration
Keep an updated allergy list (amoxicillin-clavulanate caused urticarial rash) and share with all clinicians

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms, functional impact, pain pattern, onset/age, progression, prior diagnoses, and current meds to frame likely MONA vs mimics.

Screen for red flags and alternate etiologies (fever, rapidly progressive swelling/redness, severe night pain, weight loss, neurologic symptoms, inability to bear weight/use limb, recent infection/trauma).
Clarify allergy history and reaction details (must capture amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash) and confirm current medication list including OTC NSAIDs/acetaminophen.
Order baseline tests to assess inflammation/organ function and rule out mimics prior to treatment planning (e.g., CBC, CMP/creatinine, ESR/CRP; consider RF/anti-CCP if not previously done).
Review results to ensure no strong evidence of infection/systemic inflammatory disease and to confirm kidney/liver function for safe analgesic selection.
State working diagnosis (multicentric osteolysis, nodulosis, and arthropathy) and acknowledge differential/mimics if data are incomplete.
Explain diagnosis using required elements (rare osteolysis-driven disorder, seronegative pattern vs RA, role of genetics, goals are symptom control/function not cure).
Critical safety check before prescribing any medication; confirm allergies and document amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing; ensure no interactions with current meds (e.g., anticoagulants, other NSAIDs, nephrotoxic agents).
Assess contraindications to NSAIDs/acetaminophen (GI ulcer/bleed, CKD, uncontrolled HTN, heart failure, liver disease, pregnancy, anticoagulant use).
Critical safety check before prescribing; confirm no contraindications for chosen analgesic strategy.
Provide a safe first-line symptom-control regimen (acetaminophen and/or a single NSAID if appropriate) with clear PRN vs scheduled guidance and max daily doses.
Explain treatment plan: pain control, PT/OT, splints/assistive devices; NSAID risks and safe use; avoid multiple NSAIDs; hydration; monitoring plan and imaging follow-up.
Address concerns: red flags requiring urgent care; set expectations about chronicity/monitoring; discuss uncertainty and need to rule out infection/other inflammatory disease if course changes.

Lifestyle and safety counseling: joint protection/activity modification, adherence to PT/OT home plan, limit alcohol if applicable, maintain hydration, keep updated allergy list and share with clinicians.
Arrange specialist follow-up (rheumatology, genetics, orthopedics) and plan for imaging/monitoring; provide timeframe and return precautions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, questions, and safety-net instructions.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 67-year-old man, cooperative and clear. Start by saying: “I’ve been feeling wiped out and short of breath even doing simple stuff.” Volunteer early: fatigue for a few weeks getting worse, short of breath climbing one flight of stairs, occasional heart racing (esp. standing/walking), and family says you look paler. If asked, add: dizziness on standing, dull off-and-on headaches, weakness/low stamina at work, cold hands/feet, lower appetite, and occasional chest tightness only when very winded that resolves with rest. Do not mention unless specifically asked: craving/chewing ice, sore tongue/small mouth sores, easy bruising. Mention the intermittent dry cough and vague chest pressure if the clinician explores lungs/clots—sound worried but not dramatic. If asked about bleeding, firmly deny heavy periods (you are male); if the clinician asks again, say you think they misunderstood. Express cost concerns: prefer “just vitamins,” want as few labs as possible, think bone marrow tests are optional/too expensive. If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and stay engaged, but still ask about cheaper options.

Bone marrow biopsy (summary).normal_range: Normocellular for age with trilineage hematopoiesis

correct_diagnosis.primary: Aregenerative anemia (hypoproliferative anemia with reticulocytopenia)
acceptable_alternatives: Bone marrow failure syndrome (e.g., aplastic anemia) pending workup
required_evidence: Low hemoglobin/hematocrit with low RBC count consistent with anemia
Reticulocyte % and absolute reticulocyte count inappropriately low for the degree of anemia (reticulocytopenia)
No laboratory pattern suggesting robust marrow response (i.e., no elevated reticulocyte count)
RBC indices (MCV/MCHC/RDW) used to further characterize anemia type (often normocytic/normochromic early)

expected_lab_results.MCV: Usually normal (normocytic) but may vary depending on cause
expected_lab_results.RDW: Normal to mildly increased (may rise with mixed etiologies)
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte %: Low or inappropriately normal (not elevated despite anemia)

Identify and treat underlying cause of aregenerative anemia (e.g., evaluate for marrow suppression/failure, chronic disease/inflammation, renal disease/low EPO, nutritional deficiencies) with targeted testing and specialist referral
Hematology referral for further evaluation (consider bone marrow biopsy if persistent/severe reticulocytopenia or pancytopenia suspected)
Avoid non-essential medications that can suppress marrow pending evaluation
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck CBC and reticulocyte count within 24-48 hours after transfusion or sooner if symptoms worsen; hematology follow-up within 1 week (urgent same-day if severe anemia or concerning features).

must_include: Anemia means low red blood cells/hemoglobin causing reduced oxygen delivery
Reticulocyte results suggest the bone marrow is not making enough new red blood cells (aregenerative/hypoproliferative pattern)
This pattern requires evaluation for the underlying cause rather than assuming bleeding alone
Transfusion is used to relieve symptoms or treat severe anemia; goal is symptom improvement and safe hemoglobin level
Discuss transfusion risks (reaction, fever, allergy, rare serious reactions) and monitoring during/after
Plan to investigate and treat the underlying cause to prevent recurrence
Review red-flag symptoms requiring urgent care (chest pain, fainting, worsening shortness of breath, black/bloody stools, severe weakness)
Invite questions and confirm understanding of why further testing/referral is needed
Activity guidance: pace exertion until anemia improves; avoid heavy exertion if symptomatic
Medication safety: avoid starting new OTC drugs (especially NSAIDs) without guidance until anemia cause clarified

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit anemia symptoms, severity, timeline, bleeding history, diet/alcohol, medications (incl. NSAIDs), chronic disease symptoms, infection/inflammation, pregnancy status if relevant, and red flags to triage urgency.

Order initial evaluation for aregenerative/hypoproliferative anemia: CBC with indices, reticulocyte count, peripheral smear, iron studies (ferritin, iron, TIBC/transferrin saturation), B12/folate, CMP/creatinine, TSH, hemolysis labs if uncertainty (LDH, haptoglobin, bilirubin), and inflammatory markers if indicated.
Confirm low Hgb/Hct with low reticulocyte response and review indices/smear patterns to narrow etiology (iron deficiency, B12/folate deficiency, CKD, inflammation, marrow disorder).
State working diagnosis: aregenerative/hypoproliferative anemia pattern; avoid attributing solely to bleeding without supportive evidence.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis meaning and reticulocyte implication; explain transfusion role/risks/monitoring if severe/symptomatic; address red flags and need for further testing/referral; lifestyle and medication safety guidance.

If planning transfusion or other orders with contraindications, ensure appropriate pre-order safety checks and prerequisites (type and screen/crossmatch; consider pregnancy test if applicable; consider EPO level or additional tests based on suspected cause).
Perform contraindication/safety check before ordering high-risk interventions (e.g., transfusion, IV iron, erythropoiesis-stimulating agents) per benchmark requirement.
Perform allergy check before prescribing any medication (e.g., oral iron, B12, folate) per benchmark requirement.

Initiate cause-directed therapy when supported by results (e.g., iron deficiency, B12/folate deficiency) and/or symptomatic management plan; avoid empiric therapy that could obscure diagnosis unless clinically justified.
Arrange timely follow-up to review results, monitor hemoglobin/reticulocyte response, and ensure referral (hematology) if severe anemia, pancytopenia, abnormal smear, or unclear cause.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.
